title: Anti-clockwise boundary traversal with strict duplicates
description: Print the boundary traversal of a binary tree in anti-clockwise order ensuring duplicate nodes are not repeated

examples.explanation: Step 1: Collect left boundary (non-leaves): Start at root 1 (not leaf), go left to 2 (not leaf), go left to 4 (leaf, stop). Left boundary list: [1, 2]. Step 2: Collect leaves via inorder traversal: Visit 4 (leaf) → add 4, visit 2 (not leaf), visit 7 (leaf) → add 7, visit 5 (not leaf), visit 8 (leaf) → add 8, visit 1 (not leaf), visit 3 (not leaf), visit 6 (leaf) → add 6. Leaves list: [4, 7, 8, 6]. Step 3: Collect right boundary (non-leaves, reverse): Start at root 1 (already in left boundary, exclude), go right to 3 (not leaf), go right to 6 (leaf, stop). Right boundary list: [3] (reverse order not needed as only one node). Step 4: Merge with duplicate filtering: Initialize result = [], visited = set(). Add left boundary: 1 → visited={1}, result=[1]; 2 → visited={1,2}, result=[1,2]. Add leaves: 4 → not in visited, visited={1,2,4}, result=[1,2,4]; 7 → visited={1,2,4,7}, result=[1,2,4,7]; 8 → visited={1,2,4,7,8}, result=[1,2,4,7,8]; 6 → visited={1,2,4,7,8,6}, result=[1,2,4,7,8,6]. Add right boundary: 3 → not in visited, visited={1,2,4,7,8,6,3}, result=[1,2,4,7,8,6,3]. Final output: 1 2 4 7 8 6 3.

Step 1: Collect left boundary: Start at 1 (not leaf), go left to 2 (not leaf), go left to 4 (leaf, stop). Left boundary list: [1, 2]. Step 2: Collect leaves: Inorder traversal: 4 (leaf) → add 4, 2 (not leaf), 1 (not leaf), 3 (not leaf), 5 (leaf) → add 5. Leaves list: [4, 5]. Step 3: Collect right boundary: Start at 1 (exclude as in left boundary), go right to 3 (not leaf), go right to 5 (leaf, stop). Right boundary list: [3]. Step 4: Merge: visited={}, result=[]. Add left: 1 → visited={1}, result=[1]; 2 → visited={1,2}, result=[1,2]. Add leaves: 4 → visited={1,2,4}, result=[1,2,4]; 5 → visited={1,2,4,5}, result=[1,2,4,5]. Add right: 3 → visited={1,2,4,5,3}, result=[1,2,4,5,3]. Final output: 1 2 4 5 3.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursive Collect and Filter
approaches.summary: Traverse the tree recursively to collect all boundary nodes in separate lists for left boundary, leaves, and right boundary, then merge them while filtering duplicates using a set. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to multiple passes and extra storage.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Anti-clockwise Boundary Traversal with Strict Duplicates
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force recursive collect and filter approach
ladders.explanation: This problem requires printing the boundary nodes of a binary tree in anti-clockwise order while ensuring no node appears more than once in the output. The anti-clockwise order means we traverse: 1) Left boundary from root to leftmost leaf (excluding leaves), 2) All leaf nodes from left to right, 3) Right boundary from rightmost leaf to root (excluding leaves and root if already included). The brute force approach works by collecting nodes in three separate lists through recursive traversal, then merging them while filtering duplicates using a set. The intuition is that boundary traversal can be decomposed into three distinct components that can be collected independently. We first traverse the left boundary by following left children until reaching a leaf, collecting non-leaf nodes. Then we perform an inorder traversal to collect all leaf nodes. Finally, we traverse the right boundary by following right children until reaching a leaf, collecting non-leaf nodes in reverse order. The challenge is avoiding duplicates where boundaries intersect (particularly at leaves and the root). We solve this by using a set to track visited nodes during the merging phase. The algorithm has O(n) time complexity since we visit each node a constant number of times, and O(n) space complexity for storing the lists and set. This approach is straightforward but inefficient due to multiple passes and extra storage. Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of collecting boundary nodes recursively. Common mistakes include: including leaves in left/right boundaries, incorrect order for right boundary, forgetting to exclude root from right boundary if already in left boundary, and not handling single-node trees correctly. Real code pattern: define TreeNode class with val, left, right; implement three helper functions for left boundary, leaves, and right boundary; collect results; merge with duplicate filtering.

examples.trace: Step 1: Collect left boundary (non-leaves): Start at root 1 (not leaf), go left to 2 (not leaf), go left to 4 (leaf, stop). Left boundary list: [1, 2]. Step 2: Collect leaves via inorder traversal: Visit 4 (leaf) → add 4, visit 2 (not leaf), visit 7 (leaf) → add 7, visit 5 (not leaf), visit 8 (leaf) → add 8, visit 1 (not leaf), visit 3 (not leaf), visit 6 (leaf) → add 6. Leaves list: [4, 7, 8, 6]. Step 3: Collect right boundary (non-leaves, reverse): Start at root 1 (already in left boundary, exclude), go right to 3 (not leaf), go right to 6 (leaf, stop). Right boundary list: [3] (reverse order not needed as only one node). Step 4: Merge with duplicate filtering: Initialize result = [], visited = set(). Add left boundary: 1 → visited={1}, result=[1]; 2 → visited={1,2}, result=[1,2]. Add leaves: 4 → not in visited, visited={1,2,4}, result=[1,2,4]; 7 → visited={1,2,4,7}, result=[1,2,4,7]; 8 → visited={1,2,4,7,8}, result=[1,2,4,7,8]; 6 → visited={1,2,4,7,8,6}, result=[1,2,4,7,8,6]. Add right boundary: 3 → not in visited, visited={1,2,4,7,8,6,3}, result=[1,2,4,7,8,6,3]. Final output: 1 2 4 7 8 6 3.

Step 1: Collect left boundary: Start at 1 (not leaf), go left to 2 (not leaf), go left to 4 (leaf, stop). Left boundary list: [1, 2]. Step 2: Collect leaves: Inorder traversal: 4 (leaf) → add 4, 2 (not leaf), 1 (not leaf), 3 (not leaf), 5 (leaf) → add 5. Leaves list: [4, 5]. Step 3: Collect right boundary: Start at 1 (exclude as in left boundary), go right to 3 (not leaf), go right to 5 (leaf, stop). Right boundary list: [3]. Step 4: Merge: visited={}, result=[]. Add left: 1 → visited={1}, result=[1]; 2 → visited={1,2}, result=[1,2]. Add leaves: 4 → visited={1,2,4}, result=[1,2,4]; 5 → visited={1,2,4,5}, result=[1,2,4,5]. Add right: 3 → visited={1,2,4,5,3}, result=[1,2,4,5,3]. Final output: 1 2 4 5 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Collection of Boundary Nodes
ladders.desc: Implement three recursive functions to collect left boundary, leaves, and right boundary nodes
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine involves implementing three separate recursive functions that form the core of the boundary traversal algorithm. The intuition is that the boundary can be decomposed into three distinct parts that can be collected independently through different traversal strategies. For the left boundary, we need to collect non-leaf nodes by always moving to the left child if it exists, otherwise to the right child, until we reach a leaf. This ensures we follow the outermost left path. For leaves, we perform a standard inorder traversal (though preorder or postorder would also work) and collect nodes that have no children. For the right boundary, we similarly follow right children (or left if right doesn't exist) to collect non-leaf nodes, but we need to collect them in reverse order from bottom to top, which can be achieved by adding nodes after recursive calls. Each function takes a node and a list as parameters, recursively traverses the tree, and appends appropriate nodes to the list. Time complexity for each function is O(n) in worst case as we may visit all nodes, and space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack where h is tree height. Connection to L0: These three functions provide the raw boundary node lists that are then merged in the full solution. Common mistakes: including leaves in left/right boundaries, not handling cases where a node has only one child correctly, incorrect base cases for recursion, and not maintaining the correct order for right boundary. Real code pattern: def getLeftBoundary(node, result): if not node or (not node.left and not node.right): return; result.append(node.val); if node.left: getLeftBoundary(node.left, result); else: getLeftBoundary(node.right, result).

examples.trace: Step 1: Call getLeftBoundary(root=1, result=[]). Node 1 is not null and not leaf (has children). Append 1 → result=[1]. Node has left child 2, so recursively call getLeftBoundary(2, result). Step 2: Node 2 is not null and not leaf (has children). Append 2 → result=[1,2]. Node has left child 4, so call getLeftBoundary(4, result). Step 3: Node 4 is not null but is leaf (no children). Base case returns without appending. Step 4: Unwind recursion, final result=[1,2].

Step 1: Call getLeaves(root=1, result=[]). Node 1 is not leaf, so recursively: call getLeaves(2, result). Step 2: Node 2 not leaf, recursively: call getLeaves(4, result). Step 3: Node 4 is leaf (no children), append 4 → result=[4]. Return to node 2, call getLeaves(5, result). Step 4: Node 5 is leaf, append 5 → result=[4,5]. Return to node 2, then to node 1, call getLeaves(3, result). Step 5: Node 3 not leaf, call getLeaves(6, result). Step 6: Node 6 is leaf, append 6 → result=[4,5,6]. Final result=[4,5,6].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decomposing Boundary into Three Components
ladders.desc: Understanding how to split boundary traversal into left boundary, leaves, and right boundary
ladders.explanation: The core insight for solving boundary traversal problems is recognizing that the boundary can be logically divided into three distinct components: the left boundary (non-leaf nodes on the leftmost path from root to leftmost leaf), all leaf nodes (in left-to-right order), and the right boundary (non-leaf nodes on the rightmost path from rightmost leaf to root, in bottom-up order). This decomposition works because these three sets collectively contain all boundary nodes without overlap except at intersection points (leaves and potentially the root). The left boundary is obtained by starting at the root and repeatedly going to the left child if it exists, otherwise to the right child, until reaching a leaf. This follows the outermost left edge. Leaves are collected through any complete tree traversal (inorder works naturally for left-to-right order). The right boundary is similar to left boundary but follows right children and must be collected in reverse order to maintain anti-clockwise direction. The intuition behind this decomposition is that any boundary node must belong to one of these three categories: it's either on the extreme left path, on the extreme right path, or a leaf node. By collecting these separately, we ensure we get all boundary nodes. The challenge is avoiding duplicates where components intersect: leaves appear in both leaf collection and potentially as endpoints of left/right boundaries, and the root may appear in both left and right boundaries. This is resolved in the merging phase. Time and space for this conceptual decomposition are O(1) as it's just an idea, but implementing it leads to O(n) operations. Connection to L1: This logic informs the design of the three recursive collection functions. Common mistakes: thinking boundary includes only leftmost and rightmost nodes, forgetting about leaves, incorrect ordering of components, and not considering single-node edge cases. Real code pattern: This is a conceptual step, but pseudocode would outline: result = left_boundary + leaves + reversed(right_boundary).

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify left boundary: Start at root 1, follow left to 2, follow left to 4 (leaf, stop). Non-leaf nodes: 1,2. Step 2: Identify leaves: Perform inorder traversal: visit 4 (leaf), 2 (not leaf), 5 (leaf), 1 (not leaf), 3 (not leaf), 6 (leaf). Leaves: 4,5,6. Step 3: Identify right boundary: Start at root 1, follow right to 3, follow right to 6 (leaf, stop). Non-leaf nodes: 3 (root 1 excluded as start of left boundary). Right boundary nodes: [3]. Step 4: Verify all boundary nodes covered: Boundary nodes are 1,2,4,5,6,3. Left boundary gives 1,2; leaves give 4,5,6; right gives 3. All nodes accounted for.

Step 1: Left boundary: Start at 1, follow left to 2 (leaf, stop). Non-leaf: 1. Step 2: Leaves: Inorder: 2 (leaf), 1 (not leaf), 3 (leaf). Leaves: 2,3. Step 3: Right boundary: Start at 1, follow right to 3 (leaf, stop). Non-leaf nodes: none (root 1 already in left boundary). Right boundary: []. Step 4: Verify: Boundary nodes are 1,2,3. Left gives 1, leaves give 2,3, right gives none. All covered.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Node Classification
ladders.desc: Traversing a binary tree and classifying nodes as leaf or non-leaf
ladders.explanation: This building block involves traversing a binary tree (using any standard traversal like inorder, preorder, or postorder) and for each node, determining whether it is a leaf node (both children are null) or a non-leaf node (has at least one child). This skill is fundamental because boundary traversal requires different handling of leaves versus non-leaves. The intuition is that by traversing the entire tree and examining each node's children, we can build lists or perform actions based on node type. For example, in boundary traversal, we collect leaves separately from boundary non-leaves. A recursive traversal works naturally: visit a node, check if it's a leaf by verifying both left and right are null, then recursively traverse left and right subtrees. The order of traversal determines the order in which nodes are processed; for leaves, inorder gives left-to-right order. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once, and space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal with classification enables collecting leaves and identifying non-leaf boundary nodes. Common mistakes: incorrect base case for recursion, misclassifying nodes with one child as leaves, not handling null trees, and inefficiency from multiple traversals. Real code pattern: def traverse(node): if not node: return; if not node.left and not node.right: # leaf processing; else: # non-leaf processing; traverse(node.left); traverse(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root 1. Node 1 has left child 2 and right child 3 → non-leaf. Recursively traverse left subtree (node 2). Step 2: Node 2 has no children → leaf. Add to leaves list: [2]. Return to node 1. Step 3: Process node 1 as non-leaf (already identified). Recursively traverse right subtree (node 3). Step 4: Node 3 has no children → leaf. Add to leaves list: [2,3]. Step 5: End traversal. Leaves: [2,3], Non-leaves: [1].

Step 1: Start at root 1. Node 1 has children → non-leaf, add to non-leaves: [1]. Traverse left to node 2. Step 2: Node 2 has children → non-leaf, add: [1,2]. Traverse left to node 4. Step 3: Node 4 no children → leaf, add to leaves: [4]. Return to node 2, traverse right to node 5. Step 4: Node 5 no children → leaf, add: [4,5]. Return to node 1, traverse right to node 3. Step 5: Node 3 no children → leaf, add: [4,5,3]. End. Leaves: [4,5,3], Non-leaves: [1,2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking if a Binary Tree Node is a Leaf
ladders.desc: Determining whether a given node in a binary tree has no children
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves examining a binary tree node and returning true if it is a leaf node (i.e., it has no children), or false otherwise. A leaf node is defined as a node where both the left and right child pointers are null (or None in Python). This is a fundamental operation in tree algorithms because leaf nodes often require special handling, such as in boundary traversal where leaves are collected separately. The intuition is simple: if a node has no descendants, it's a leaf. The operation is performed by checking if node.left is null and node.right is null. It's typically implemented as a helper function or inline condition. Time complexity is O(1) since it involves only constant-time pointer checks, and space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used within tree traversal to classify nodes as leaves or non-leaves. Common mistakes: considering nodes with one child as leaves (they are not, as they have a descendant), not handling null nodes correctly (a null node is not a leaf, it's an empty subtree), and confusion with root node (the root can be a leaf if it has no children). Real code pattern: def isLeaf(node): return node is not None and node.left is None and node.right is None.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if node is null: node is 1, not null. Step 2: Check left child: node.left is 2, not null. Step 3: Since left is not null, node is not a leaf. Return false. (Note: we don't need to check right because both must be null for leaf.)

Step 1: Check if node is null: node is 2, not null. Step 2: Check left child: node.left is null. Step 3: Check right child: node.right is null. Step 4: Both left and right are null, so node is a leaf. Return true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Null Checks
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic structure of a binary tree node and how to check for null/none values
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the fundamental building block of binary trees: the node structure. A binary tree node typically contains a value (e.g., an integer) and two pointers/references to its left and right children. In many programming languages, this is represented as a class or struct with fields for value, left, and right. The left and right pointers can be null (or None) to indicate the absence of a child. Understanding this structure is crucial because all tree algorithms start from accessing nodes and traversing via these pointers. The key skill here is checking whether a node or its children are null before performing operations to avoid null pointer errors. The intuition is that null represents an empty subtree, and algorithms must handle these cases gracefully. For example, when traversing, if a node is null, we simply return without further processing. This concept is prerequisite to all tree operations. Time and space are not applicable as this is about data structure definition. Connection to L4: Null checks are used in the leaf-checking operation to ensure safe access. Common mistakes: forgetting to check for null before accessing node fields, assuming a node exists when it might be null, and confusion between null node and node with null children. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val=0, left=None, right=None): self.val = val; self.left = left; self.right = right. To check: if node is None: # handle empty case.

examples.output: Node is not null, left is null, right is null
examples.trace: Step 1: Examine the node: it has value 1, so it is not null. Step 2: Check left pointer: it points to null, meaning no left child. Step 3: Check right pointer: it points to null, meaning no right child. Step 4: Conclusion: This is a valid node with no children.

Step 1: The reference to the node is null (e.g., None in Python). Step 2: This means there is no node object. Step 3: Cannot access any fields like val, left, or right without causing an error. Step 4: In algorithms, we typically handle this with a condition like if node is None: return.

testCases.expected: Node val=1, left not null (val=2), right=null
Node val=1, left=null, right not null (val=3)
Node val=1, left not null (val=2), right not null (val=3)

testCases.input: Check if node is null: node=null
Check if node is null: node=TreeNode(1)
Check left child: node=TreeNode(1, null, null)
Check left child: node=TreeNode(1, TreeNode(2), null)
Check right child: node=TreeNode(1, null, TreeNode(3))

approaches.name: Approach 2: Single-Pass Iterative with Stack and Set
approaches.summary: Perform an anti-clockwise traversal using a stack to process nodes in order: root, left boundary, leaves, and right boundary, while using a set to skip duplicates on the fly. This method avoids separate collections by integrating checks during traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Anti-clockwise Boundary Traversal with Strict Duplicates
ladders.desc: Complete solution using single-pass iterative traversal with stack and set to avoid duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires printing the boundary nodes of a binary tree in anti-clockwise order (root → left boundary → leaves → right boundary) without repeating any node. The single-pass iterative approach elegantly combines all boundary components into one traversal using a stack for processing order and a set for duplicate detection. Intuition: By processing nodes in the exact anti-clockwise sequence, we can output them immediately while checking a visited set to skip duplicates. This avoids storing separate collections for different boundary parts. The algorithm: 1. Initialize stack with root, visited set empty, result list empty. 2. While stack not empty: pop node, if not visited: add to result and visited set. 3. Push right child then left child (for left boundary/leaves order). 4. After left boundary/leaves, handle right boundary by traversing from root's right child upward. Time: O(n) visiting each node once. Space: O(n) for stack/set. Connection to L1: This integrates the boundary traversal logic with duplicate checking. Common mistakes: Forgetting to handle right boundary separately, incorrect stack push order, or not checking visited before output. Real pattern: Use stack for DFS-like traversal with specific child order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[1], visited={}, result=[]. Pop 1 → not visited → result=[1], visited={1}. Push 3 then 2 → stack=[3,2]. Step 2: Pop 2 → not visited → result=[1,2], visited={1,2}. Push 5 then 4 → stack=[3,5,4]. Step 3: Pop 4 → not visited → result=[1,2,4], visited={1,2,4}. No children → stack=[3,5]. Step 4: Pop 5 → not visited → result=[1,2,4,5], visited={1,2,4,5}. No children → stack=[3]. Step 5: Pop 3 → not visited → result=[1,2,4,5,3], visited={1,2,4,5,3}. Push 7 then 6 → stack=[7,6]. Step 6: Pop 6 → not visited → result=[1,2,4,5,3,6], visited={1,2,4,5,3,6}. No children → stack=[7]. Step 7: Pop 7 → not visited → result=[1,2,4,5,3,6,7], visited={1,2,4,5,3,6,7}. No children → stack empty. Right boundary: root's right child 3 already visited → done. Final: 1 2 4 5 6 7 3.

Step 1: Stack=[1], visited={}, result=[]. Pop 1 → not visited → result=[1], visited={1}. Push 2 (right) then 2 (left) → stack=[2,2]. Step 2: Pop top 2 → not visited → result=[1,2], visited={1,2}. Push 3 then null → stack=[2,3]. Step 3: Pop 3 → not visited → result=[1,2,3], visited={1,2,3}. Push null then 4 → stack=[2,4]. Step 4: Pop 4 → not visited → result=[1,2,3,4], visited={1,2,3,4}. No children → stack=[2]. Step 5: Pop 2 → already visited → skip. Stack empty. Right boundary: root's right child 2 already visited → done. Final: 1 2 3 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative Boundary Traversal with Stack
ladders.desc: Perform anti-clockwise boundary traversal using stack without duplicate handling.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on traversing the boundary in correct order using a stack, which is the core of the full solution. Intuition: A stack allows LIFO processing to simulate the anti-clockwise order: we push right child then left child to process left boundary and leaves first, then handle right boundary separately. The algorithm: 1. Initialize stack with root. 2. While stack not empty: pop node, output it. 3. If node has right child, push it. 4. If node has left child, push it (this order ensures left boundary before right in traversal). 5. After stack empty, traverse right boundary from root's right child upward (excluding root and leaves). Time: O(n) as each node pushed/popped once. Space: O(n) for stack. Connection to L0: This provides the traversal order; adding a visited set in L0 prevents duplicates. Common mistakes: Incorrect push order (should be right then left), forgetting right boundary traversal, or including leaves in right boundary. Real pattern: Use stack for controlled DFS with specific child order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[1]. Pop 1 → output 1. Push 3 then 2 → stack=[3,2]. Step 2: Pop 2 → output 2. Push 5 then 4 → stack=[3,5,4]. Step 3: Pop 4 → output 4. No children → stack=[3,5]. Step 4: Pop 5 → output 5. No children → stack=[3]. Step 5: Pop 3 → output 3. Push 7 then 6 → stack=[7,6]. Step 6: Pop 6 → output 6. No children → stack=[7]. Step 7: Pop 7 → output 7. Stack empty. Right boundary: root's right child 3 already output → done. Final: 1 2 4 5 6 7 3.

Step 1: Stack=[1]. Pop 1 → output 1. Push null then 2 → stack=[2]. Step 2: Pop 2 → output 2. Push 4 then 3 → stack=[4,3]. Step 3: Pop 3 → output 3. No children → stack=[4]. Step 4: Pop 4 → output 4. Stack empty. Right boundary: root's right child null → done. Final: 1 2 3 4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Anti-clockwise Order Definition and Processing
ladders.desc: Understand and implement the sequence: root, left boundary, leaves, right boundary.
ladders.explanation: This level defines the anti-clockwise boundary order, which is crucial for the traversal. Intuition: Imagine walking around the tree counter-clockwise: start at root, go down left boundary, collect leaves left-to-right, then go up right boundary. The left boundary includes nodes from root to leftmost leaf, excluding leaves. The right boundary includes nodes from rightmost leaf to root, excluding root and leaves. Leaves are all nodes with no children. The algorithm: 1. Output root. 2. Traverse left boundary: while node has left child, go left; if no left but has right, go right (still boundary). 3. Output leaves via inorder traversal. 4. Traverse right boundary: from root's right child, while node has right child, go right; if no right but has left, go left. Time: O(n) traversing each node once. Space: O(1) if iterative. Connection to L1: This order dictates stack push/pop sequence. Common mistakes: Including leaves in left/right boundaries, incorrect leaf order (should be left-to-right), or missing root in output. Real pattern: Separate functions for left boundary, leaves, right boundary.

examples.trace: Step 1: Root: 1. Step 2: Left boundary: from 1, left child 2 → output 2. From 2, left child 4 → output 4 (leaf, so stop left boundary). Step 3: Leaves: inorder gives 4,5,6,7 → output 5,6,7. Step 4: Right boundary: from root's right child 3, right child 7 → output 7 (leaf, so stop). But 3 not output yet → output 3. Final: 1 2 4 5 6 7 3.

Step 1: Root: 1. Step 2: Left boundary: from 1, left child 2 → output 2. From 2, left child 3 → output 3 (leaf). Step 3: Leaves: inorder gives 3,4 → output 4. Step 4: Right boundary: root's right child null → none. Final: 1 2 3 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Traversal with Stack (DFS Variant)
ladders.desc: Use stack to traverse tree in a custom order (not standard inorder/preorder/postorder).
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using a stack to traverse a binary tree in a specific order, which is foundational for the boundary traversal. Intuition: A stack allows manual control over traversal sequence by pushing children in reverse order of desired processing. Unlike recursion, it offers flexibility to mix orders. The algorithm: 1. Push root to stack. 2. While stack not empty: pop node, process it. 3. Push children to stack in reverse order of desired traversal (e.g., push right then left for left-first). Time: O(n) each node processed once. Space: O(n) stack size. Connection to L2: This enables implementing the anti-clockwise order by customizing push order. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check for null children, infinite loops from repushing nodes, or incorrect push order. Real pattern: Standard iterative DFS with stack, but with custom child order.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (2L, 3R), traverse with stack pushing right then left
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[1]. Pop 1 → process 1. Push 3 then 2 → stack=[3,2]. Step 2: Pop 2 → process 2. No children → stack=[3]. Step 3: Pop 3 → process 3. Stack empty. Final: 1 2 3.

examples.input: Tree: 1 (2L, 3R), 2 (4L, 5R), traverse with stack pushing left then right
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[1]. Pop 1 → process 1. Push 2 then 3 → stack=[2,3]. Step 2: Pop 3 → process 3. No children → stack=[2]. Step 3: Pop 2 → process 2. Push 5 then 4 → stack=[5,4]. Step 4: Pop 4 → process 4. No children → stack=[5]. Step 5: Pop 5 → process 5. Stack empty. Final: 1 3 2 5 4.

testCases.input: Tree: null, push right then left
Tree: 1 (2L, null), 2 (3L, 4R), push right then left
Tree: 1 (2L, null), 2 (3L, 4R), push left then right

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Push and Pop with Stack for Tree Nodes
ladders.desc: Fundamental stack operations applied to tree node objects.
ladders.explanation: This level covers the basic stack operations (push and pop) when working with tree nodes, which is essential for iterative traversal. Intuition: A stack is a LIFO data structure; pushing adds nodes to the top, popping removes from the top. For trees, we push nodes to process later, controlling order. The algorithm: 1. Initialize empty stack. 2. Push root: stack.append(root). 3. Pop node: node = stack.pop(). 4. Process node (e.g., print value). 5. Push children if they exist. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(n) for stack storage. Connection to L3: These operations enable custom traversal orders. Common mistakes: Popping from empty stack, pushing null nodes, or not using stack correctly for LIFO. Real pattern: Standard stack usage in Python with list.

examples.input: Push root 1, pop it, push children 2 and 3, pop them
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[]. Push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 2: Pop → node=1, process 1. Push 2 then 3 → stack=[2,3]. Step 3: Pop → node=3, process 3. Step 4: Pop → node=2, process 2. Stack empty. Final: 1 3 2.

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack=[]. Push 1 → stack=[1]. Step 2: Pop → node=1, process 1. Push 2 → stack=[2]. Step 3: Pop → node=2, process 2. Stack empty. Final: 1 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Basic Access
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree node is and how to access its value and children.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces binary tree nodes, which are the basic units for the problem. Intuition: A binary tree node typically has a value and references to left and right children. In code, it's often a class with attributes: val, left, right. The algorithm: 1. Define node class: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. 2. Create nodes and link them to form a tree. 3. Access node value: node.val. 4. Access children: node.left, node.right. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(1) per node. Connection to L4: Nodes are pushed/popped from stack. Common mistakes: Assuming children exist without null checks, incorrect linking, or misunderstanding tree structure. Real pattern: Standard node definition in Python.

examples.output: Node values: 1, left child 2, right child 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Create nodes: node1=Node(1), node2=Node(2), node3=Node(3). Step 2: Link: node1.left=node2; node1.right=node3. Step 3: Access: node1.val=1, node1.left.val=2, node1.right.val=3.

examples.input: Create tree: root=1, left=2 (no right child)
examples.output: Node values: 1, left child 2, right child null
examples.trace: Step 1: Create nodes: node1=Node(1), node2=Node(2). Step 2: Link: node1.left=node2; node1.right=None. Step 3: Access: node1.val=1, node1.left.val=2, node1.right is None.

Node with val=15, right child val=30

approaches.name: Approach 3: Optimized Morris-like Traversal with O(1) Extra Space
approaches.summary: Modify Morris traversal to visit boundary nodes in anti-clockwise order without using a stack or recursion, by threading the tree and tracking visited nodes via parent pointers to avoid duplicates. This approach minimizes space usage but increases implementation complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Anti-clockwise Boundary Traversal with O(1) Space
ladders.desc: Complete Morris-like traversal to print boundary nodes in anti-clockwise order without repeating duplicates
ladders.explanation: This solution implements a modified Morris traversal that visits boundary nodes in anti-clockwise order while using O(1) extra space. The anti-clockwise order consists of: 1) Left boundary (top-down), 2) Leaf nodes (left to right), 3) Right boundary (bottom-up). The challenge is avoiding duplicate nodes at corners where boundaries meet. The Morris technique threads the tree by creating temporary links from predecessor nodes to current nodes, allowing traversal without stack/recursion. We track visited nodes using parent pointers and a visited flag system to prevent duplicates. The algorithm first traverses the left boundary from root to leftmost leaf, printing nodes. Then it performs an inorder-like traversal to find all leaf nodes, printing them. Finally, it traverses the right boundary from bottom to top, printing nodes in reverse order. Throughout, we maintain a visited set using parent pointers and node marking to ensure no node is printed twice. The time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited a constant number of times, and space is O(1) excluding the tree itself. This approach is complex due to the threading logic and duplicate avoidance, but optimal for space constraints. Connection to L1: This integrates the Morris threading subroutine with boundary detection logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle single-node trees, incorrectly threading nodes, printing duplicates at boundary intersections, and infinite loops from improper thread removal. Real code pattern: Initialize current = root. While current exists: if no left child, process as leaf/boundary, move right. Else: find predecessor. If predecessor's right is null, thread it to current, process left boundary if applicable, move left. Else: remove thread, process as leaf if applicable, move right. Finally traverse right boundary bottom-up.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at root 1. Print left boundary: 1. Move to left child 2.
Step 2: At node 2. Print left boundary: 2. Move to left child 4.
Step 3: At node 4 (leaf). Print leaf: 4. Backtrack via threading.
Step 4: Return to node 2. Right child 5 is leaf. Print leaf: 5.
Step 5: Move to root's right child 3. Left child 6 is leaf. Print leaf: 6.
Step 6: At node 3. Right child 7 is leaf. Print leaf: 7.
Step 7: Traverse right boundary bottom-up: 3 (already visited? Check visited flag: no, print).
Step 8: Right boundary continues to root 1 (already printed as left boundary, skip).
Final output: 1 2 4 5 6 7 3.

Step 1: Print left boundary: 1, 2, 4.
Step 2: Find leaves: Start at 4 (leaf), print 4. Move to 2's right child 5.
Step 3: At 5, left child 6 is leaf, print 6. Right child 7 is leaf, print 7.
Step 4: Move to root's right child 3 (leaf), print 3.
Step 5: Traverse right boundary bottom-up: 5 (not on left boundary, print), 2 (on left boundary, skip), 1 (root, skip).
Step 6: Final output: 1 2 4 6 7 5 3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Morris Threading for Boundary Detection
ladders.desc: Modified Morris traversal that identifies boundary nodes while threading
ladders.explanation: This subroutine adapts the standard Morris inorder traversal to detect boundary nodes (left boundary, leaves, right boundary) during traversal. The core idea is to use threading to traverse without stack/recursion while classifying nodes based on their position. For each node: if it has no left child, it's potentially a leaf or right boundary node. If it has a left child, find its inorder predecessor. If the predecessor's right pointer is null, thread it to the current node—this indicates first visit, so the node is on the left boundary if it's the leftmost node in its subtree. Move to the left child. If the predecessor's right pointer points to the current node, remove the thread—this indicates second visit, so the node has been fully processed, and if it's a leaf, print it. Move to the right child. Throughout, maintain flags to track visited nodes and avoid duplicates. The time complexity is O(n) with O(1) extra space. Connection to L0: This subroutine is the engine that drives the full solution, handling traversal and node classification. Connection to L2: This implements the threading technique for boundary traversal. Common mistakes: Not removing threads after use (causing infinite loops), misclassifying boundary nodes, and incorrect predecessor finding. Real code pattern: current = root. While current: if not current.left: process as leaf/right boundary, current = current.right. Else: pred = current.left; while pred.right and pred.right != current: pred = pred.right. If not pred.right: pred.right = current; process as left boundary if applicable; current = current.left. Else: pred.right = null; process as leaf if applicable; current = current.right.

examples.output: Left boundary: 1,2,4; Leaves: 4,5,3; Right boundary: 5,2,1 (with duplicates removed)
examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, has left child 2. Find predecessor of 1: from 2, go right until null → 5. Thread 5.right to 1. Print left boundary node 1. Move to 2.
Step 2: current=2, has left child 4. Find predecessor: from 4, go right until null → 4 itself. Thread 4.right to 2. Print left boundary node 2. Move to 4.
Step 3: current=4, no left child. Print leaf 4. Move to 4.right (threaded to 2).
Step 4: current=2, predecessor 4.right points to 2, so remove thread (4.right=null). Print leaf? 2 is not leaf (has children), skip. Move to 2.right=5.
Step 5: current=5, no left child. Print leaf 5. Move to 5.right (threaded to 1).
Step 6: current=1, predecessor 5.right points to 1, remove thread. Print leaf? 1 not leaf. Move to 1.right=3.
Step 7: current=3, no left child. Print leaf 3. Move to 3.right=null. Done.

Step 1: current=1, no left child. Print left boundary 1. Move to 1.right=2.
Step 2: current=2, no left child. Print as right boundary? Not yet—wait for bottom-up traversal later. Move to 2.right=3.
Step 3: current=3, no left child. Print leaf 3. Move to 3.right=null.
Step 4: Later, traverse right boundary bottom-up: 3,2,1 (skip duplicates).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Threading Trees for O(1) Space Traversal
ladders.desc: Using temporary links to traverse a tree without stack or recursion
ladders.explanation: Threading is a technique that modifies a tree temporarily by creating links from predecessor nodes to current nodes, allowing traversal without additional data structures. In a binary tree, for inorder traversal, the predecessor of a node is the rightmost node in its left subtree. By setting the predecessor's right pointer to the current node, we create a temporary link that acts as a 'thread' to return to the current node after visiting the left subtree. This enables us to traverse left, process nodes, and then return to the parent without recursion. The process: Start at the root. If the current node has no left child, process it and move right. If it has a left child, find its inorder predecessor. If the predecessor's right is null, thread it to the current node, then move left. If the predecessor's right points to the current node, we've visited the left subtree, so remove the thread, process the current node, and move right. This achieves O(n) time and O(1) space. The intuition is that threading simulates the call stack implicitly. Connection to L1: This logic is the foundation for the Morris subroutine. Connection to L3: This builds on tree traversal concepts. Common mistakes: Forgetting to remove threads (causing cycles), incorrect predecessor finding, and not handling null cases. Real code pattern: while current: if not current.left: process(current); current = current.right; else: pred = current.left; while pred.right and pred.right != current: pred = pred.right; if not pred.right: pred.right = current; current = current.left; else: pred.right = null; process(current); current = current.right.

examples.trace:
Step 1: current=1, has left child 2. Find predecessor: from 2, go right to 5. Thread 5.right to 1. Move to 2.
Step 2: current=2, has left child 4. Find predecessor: from 4, go right to null → 4. Thread 4.right to 2. Move to 4.
Step 3: current=4, no left child. Process 4. Move to 4.right (threaded to 2).
Step 4: current=2, predecessor 4.right points to 2, so remove thread. Process 2. Move to 2.right=5.
Step 5: current=5, no left child. Process 5. Move to 5.right (threaded to 1).
Step 6: current=1, predecessor 5.right points to 1, remove thread. Process 1. Move to 1.right=3.
Step 7: current=3, no left child. Process 3. Move to 3.right=null. Done.

Step 1: current=1, no left child. Process 1. Move to 1.right=2.
Step 2: current=2, no left child. Process 2. Move to 2.right=3.
Step 3: current=3, no left child. Process 3. Move to 3.right=null. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Inorder Predecessor in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Locate the rightmost node in the left subtree of a given node
ladders.explanation: The inorder predecessor of a node in a binary tree is the node that comes immediately before it in an inorder traversal. For a node with a left child, the predecessor is the rightmost node in its left subtree. To find it: start from the left child, then repeatedly move to the right child until the right pointer is null or points to the current node (to avoid cycles in threaded trees). This operation is key for Morris traversal because it identifies where to create or remove threads. The intuition is that in inorder traversal, after visiting the left subtree entirely, we return to the parent via this predecessor. Time complexity is O(h) in the worst case, where h is the height of the tree, but amortized O(1) over the entire traversal. Space is O(1). Connection to L2: This is used in the threading logic to set up temporary links. Connection to L4: This relies on basic tree navigation. Common mistakes: Not checking for null pointers, infinite loops if the tree has cycles, and incorrect handling of threaded links. Real code pattern: Function findPredecessor(node, current): pred = node.left; while pred.right and pred.right != current: pred = pred.right; return pred.

examples.input: Tree: Node A with left child B, B has right child C, C has no right child. Find predecessor of A.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at left child of A: B.
Step 2: Move to right child of B: C.
Step 3: C has no right child, so stop. Predecessor is C.

examples.input: Tree: Node X with left child Y, Y has no right child. Find predecessor of X.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at left child of X: Y.
Step 2: Y has no right child, so stop. Predecessor is Y.

Tree: Node(1) with left=Node(2), Node(2) has right=Node(3), Node(3) has right=Node(4), Node(4) has right=Node(3) (cycle), find predecessor of 1
Tree: Node(1) with left=Node(2), Node(2) has right=null, but Node(2).right points to 1 (threaded), find predecessor of 1
Tree: Node(1) with left=Node(2), Node(2) has right=Node(3), Node(3) has right=Node(4), Node(4) has right=Node(5), find predecessor of 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Navigating Binary Tree Nodes via Pointers
ladders.desc: Moving through a binary tree using left and right child pointers
ladders.explanation: In a binary tree, each node has pointers to its left and right children (and possibly a parent). Navigation involves starting at a node and moving to its children or parent by following these pointers. Basic operations include: moving to the left child (current = current.left), moving to the right child (current = current.right), and moving to the parent if available (current = current.parent). This is fundamental for any tree traversal algorithm. The intuition is that the tree structure is defined by these pointers, and traversal algorithms systematically visit nodes by following them. Time complexity for a single move is O(1). Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used to find predecessors by moving right repeatedly. Connection to L5: This builds on the concept of tree nodes. Common mistakes: Not checking for null pointers before dereferencing, which leads to errors, and infinite loops if pointers form cycles. Real code pattern: To move left: if current.left: current = current.left. To move right: if current.right: current = current.right. To move to parent: if current.parent: current = current.parent.

examples.input: Tree: Node A with left=B, right=C. Start at A, move left, then right from there.
examples.output: Positions: A → B → (B has no right, so null)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at node A.
Step 2: Move left: current = A.left = B.
Step 3: Move right from B: B.right is null, so cannot move.

examples.input: Tree: Node X with left=Y, Y has right=Z. Start at X, move left, then right, then right again.
examples.output: Positions: X → Y → Z → (Z has no right, null)
examples.trace:
Step 1: Start at X.
Step 2: Move left: current = Y.
Step 3: Move right: current = Y.right = Z.
Step 4: Move right from Z: Z.right is null, stop.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure
ladders.desc: Understanding the basic building block of a binary tree
ladders.explanation: A binary tree node is a fundamental data structure that represents an element in a binary tree. Each node typically contains: a value (e.g., an integer), a pointer to its left child, a pointer to its right child, and optionally a pointer to its parent. The left and right children are other nodes or null, indicating no child. This structure allows hierarchical organization of data. The intuition is that nodes are connected in a parent-child relationship, forming a tree where each node has at most two children. This is the basis for all tree algorithms. Time and space for a single node are O(1). Connection to L4: This structure enables navigation via pointers. Common mistakes: Forgetting to initialize pointers to null, misunderstanding that children are pointers to nodes not values, and creating cycles by incorrect pointer assignments. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None; self.parent = None (optional).

examples.input: Create a node with value 10, no children.
examples.trace:
Step 1: Allocate memory for a new node.
Step 2: Set val = 10.
Step 3: Set left = null.
Step 4: Set right = null.
Step 5: Node is ready.

Step 5: Set 5.right = node(7).
